The community had a hotel, a post office, and several mills at its height, serving as one of the more commercially active settlements in early DuPage County before the railroads and the growing city of Chicago absorbed the regional commerce. Today the Fullersburg Woods area along Salt Creek is a forest preserve managed by the DuPage County Forest Preserve District, with a nature center and maintained trails. The preserve's protected status means metal detecting is not permitted within its boundaries, but the rich early Illinois settlement history of the area makes it a fascinating site to visit.

name: Keystone, KS
description: Keystone was a small Kansas farming community established during the great late 19th-century settlement of the Kansas plains, named in the tradition of communities that evoked strength and centrality — the keystone being the central architectural element that locks an arch together. The community served surrounding wheat farmers and cattlemen with a church, grain elevator, and country store during the height of the Kansas agricultural boom before the combination of repeated drought, low commodity prices, and rural depopulation through the mid-20th century emptied it. Today the site is Kansas agricultural land with perhaps a grain elevator foundation or church remnant. Old elevator sites and farmstead perimeters in the Keystone area may yield late 19th-century to early 20th-century coins, farm hardware, and domestic items.

name: Clarenceville, MI
description: Clarenceville was a small Michigan community in the southeastern part of the state — now largely absorbed into the greater Detroit metropolitan area — established during the mid-19th century farming era when the rich agricultural land of Wayne County supported dozens of small market communities serving local farm families. The town served as a minor rural trading center before being engulfed by suburban expansion spreading west from Detroit through the 20th century. Today the Clarenceville area is suburban Livonia or Redford Township, with its 19th-century agricultural past buried under residential and commercial development. Original farmstead sites occasionally surface during construction, sometimes revealing early American coins, farm implements, and domestic hardware from the pre-suburban era.

name: Glastenbury, VT
description: Glastenbury was a Vermont township in Bennington County that was organized in 1834 but never successfully settled, with the forested mountain terrain of the Green Mountains proving too inhospitable for sustained farming — a pattern common in Vermont's remote hill towns where thin soils, brutal winters, and isolated terrain defeated repeated settlement attempts. A small charcoal-making and logging community briefly occupied the town in the mid-to-late 19th century, supporting a handful of families who supplied charcoal to nearby iron furnaces, but the population dropped to single digits by the early 20th century and Glastenbury became a 'ghost town' township. The area is now a designated wilderness within Green Mountain National Forest, and the remote forest terrain holds old charcoal mounds, logging-era debris, and stone foundations visible to hikers. Metal detecting is prohibited within National Forest Wilderness designations, but the area attracts hikers interested in Vermont's vanished hill town history.

name: Lewiston, VT
description: Lewiston was a Vermont hill town in Windham or Orange County that experienced the typical cycle of early 19th-century settlement optimism followed by rural abandonment as Vermont's rocky upland farms proved unsustainable against the competition of richer Midwestern agricultural land. Families who had carved farms from Vermont's forested hills in the 1800s–1840s abandoned them by the 1870s–1900s as young people emigrated to urban factories and western farms. Today the former Lewiston township is forested land with stone walls, cellar holes, and a historic cemetery as the surviving evidence of its settlement era. Vermont cellar hole sites are productive for New England domestic ceramics, hand-forged iron implements, and 19th-century coins — though most are on private or state forest land requiring permission or permit.

name: Ricker Basin, VT
description: Ricker Basin was a Vermont hill farming settlement in the Green Mountains of Washington County, occupied by the Ricker family and a small cluster of neighboring farms during the early 19th century before the steep mountain terrain, thin soils, and economic pressures of mid-century rural Vermont drove the last families out by the 1880s. The Ricker family homestead site and an associated cemetery survive in what is now the Groton State Forest, accessible by hiking trail from the Kettle Pond area. Stone foundation ruins, old stone walls, and orchard remnants mark the former farmstead locations, creating an evocative landscape that is popular with hikers and history enthusiasts. Metal detecting in Vermont state forests requires a permit, but the site is an exceptional example of Vermont's extensive ghost farm heritage.

name: Beebe River, NH
description: Beebe River was a New Hampshire lumber and mill community in Carroll County, established in the 19th century around a series of water-powered sawmills and later a clapboard mill that processed the vast timber resources of the White Mountain foothills. The town grew to support mill workers with a company store, housing, and community buildings during the height of the White Mountain timber era before the mills closed and the population dispersed in the early 20th century. Today the Beebe River area retains some structural remains from the mill era, situated on private land or within state forests of the White Mountains region. The mill pond earthworks and surrounding worker community sites may preserve early 20th-century industrial hardware, company-store tokens, and domestic artifacts.

name: Kilkenny, NH
description: Kilkenny was a New Hampshire township in Coos County — the state's wild and remote northern tip — that was granted in 1774 but never developed into a functioning community, with its dense spruce and fir forest, wet terrain, and harsh climate defeating every attempt at sustained agricultural settlement. The township today is part of the White Mountain National Forest and is crossed by the Kilkenny Ridge Trail, making it a destination for hikers rather than settlers. The occasional 19th-century logging camp or trapper's cabin site may survive in the deep forest. The national forest designation prohibits metal detecting, but the landscape preserves an authentic view of what the North Country looked like before Euro-American settlement changed it.

name: Livermore, NH
description: Livermore was a New Hampshire hill town in Grafton County, originally granted in 1876 and briefly occupied by a small logging and lumber community that harvested the vast spruce and fir forests of the Pemigewasset Wilderness during the late 19th century. The Livermore Lumber Company operated a sawmill and maintained worker housing, a railroad spur, and company facilities in the wilderness interior for several decades before the timber was exhausted and operations ceased by the early 20th century. Today the former township of Livermore is within the White Mountain National Forest, and the old railroad grades are hiking trails through the Pemi Wilderness. The national forest wilderness designation prohibits metal detecting, but the logging-era infrastructure remnants — old logging camp sites, rail spikes, and mill pond earthworks — are visible to hikers.

name: Monson, NH
description: Monson was a New Hampshire hill town in Hillsborough County that was granted in 1748 but achieved only limited settlement before the population dispersed during the 19th century as New Hampshire's upland farms proved economically unsustainable. The rocky terrain and thin soils that discouraged sustained agriculture left behind a landscape of stone walls, cellar holes, and old orchards that mark the locations of former farmsteads. Today the former Monson township is part of Greenfield State Park or adjacent private land in Hillsborough County. Stone-wall-enclosed old fields and cellar hole sites are the most productive areas for New England ghost town detectorists, who may find 18th-to-19th-century coins, domestic ceramics, and hand-forged farm implements.

name: Bartlett, ND
description: Bartlett was a small North Dakota farming community on the northern Great Plains, established during the great homestead rush of the 1890s–1910s when the federal government opened Dakota lands to settlement and thousands of families filed on 160-acre claims across the region's wheat-growing prairie. The community had a grain elevator, a general store, and a church serving surrounding homesteaders during the wheat-growing boom years before the combination of drought, low wheat prices, and the Great Depression collapsed the North Dakota homestead economy in the 1930s. Today the Bartlett area is active wheat farmland with perhaps a deteriorating elevator or church foundation as the only reminder of the community. The surrounding flat agricultural land, worked with landowner permission, may yield early 20th-century coins, homestead hardware, and farm implements.

name: Gold Point, NV
description: Gold Point was a silver and gold mining camp in Esmeralda County, Nevada, known variously as Hornsilver and Lime Point before settling on its current name, booming in the 1860s and again briefly in the early 20th century as repeated silver discoveries drew optimistic miners to the remote desert range between Goldfield and Beatty. The town had a post office, hotel, saloons, and a peak population of several hundred during its most active periods before ore depletion and the collapse of Nevada's silver economy reduced it to a handful of die-hard residents. Gold Point is today one of Nevada's most accessible ghost towns — a small but authentic collection of deteriorating adobe and wood-frame buildings still standing in the high desert — maintained by a private owner who welcomes visitors. The surrounding desert mine dumps and old claim areas are rich territory for period mining tools, ore specimens, and desert-preserved camp artifacts.

name: Nevadaville, CO
description: Nevadaville was a significant gold mining camp in Gilpin County, Colorado, established in 1859 immediately after the Pike's Peak gold rush discoveries and growing alongside Central City as one of the most productive gold camps in the territory, with lode gold deposits yielding millions in ore over the following decades. The camp had over 3,000 residents at its peak, with multiple quartz mills, a stamp mill, saloons, churches, and a school serving the hard-rock mining community until declining gold prices and ore depletion reduced the population through the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Today Nevadaville's remaining structures sit on the hillside above Central City, and portions of the old townsite are accessible via roads from the Central City gaming district. The mine dumps and old claim areas surrounding Nevadaville are rich territory for mining artifacts, miner's checks, and period tools — though access to some areas may require coordination with local mine claim holders.

name: Dearfield, CO
description: Dearfield was a pioneering African American agricultural colony in Weld County, Colorado, founded in 1910 by O.T. Jackson and other Black entrepreneurs as part of a Back-to-the-Land movement that sought to create self-sufficient Black farming communities on the Colorado plains during an era of intense racial segregation. At its peak in the early 1920s, Dearfield had nearly 700 residents, farms, a post office, a church, a grocery store, and a dance hall — an extraordinary achievement given the racial and economic barriers facing Black settlers on the high plains. The Great Depression, drought, and racial economic discrimination combined to empty the colony by the 1940s, and most structures were demolished or collapsed. Today the Dearfield site in Weld County retains a few deteriorating structures, is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, and is the subject of ongoing preservation efforts — a powerful symbol of African American pioneering on the Colorado frontier.

name: Adelaide, CO
description: Adelaide in Gunnison County was a small Colorado coal mining camp that served the coal-bearing formations of the Elk Mountain area during the late 19th and early 20th centuries, housing miners who worked the underground seams that fueled regional industries and railroad operations. The camp had company housing, a store, and basic community facilities before the mines were exhausted or became uneconomical and the camp was shuttered. Today the site is private land in Gunnison County's mountain terrain, with mine shaft collars and tailings piles marking the locations of former underground workings. Coal mining camp artifacts — brass lamp parts, mining tokens, company store merchandise — are the typical finds at such sites for detectorists with landowner access.

name: Adelaide, Lake County, CO
description: Adelaide in Lake County was a small Colorado mining camp in the high terrain near Leadville, established during the silver mining boom of the 1870s–1880s when the Leadville district became one of the richest silver fields in the American West, attracting tens of thousands of miners and supporting dozens of satellite camps across the surrounding mountains. The camp served as a minor service point and housing cluster for miners working nearby claims, lacking the full commercial infrastructure of larger Leadville-district towns. Today the Lake County site is on or near the public lands managed by the U.S. Forest Service in the San Isabel National Forest area. The high-altitude mine dumps and old camp sites of the Leadville district are among the most productive in Colorado for mining-era artifacts — with miner's tools, ore samples, and period hardware common finds.

name: Arapahoe, CO
description: Arapahoe was a small Colorado community, possibly a stagecoach stop or minor railroad watering point serving travelers crossing the eastern Colorado plains during the 1870s–1890s when the cattle and wheat economy of the high plains was just developing. The community served a limited role for surrounding homesteaders and cattle ranchers before better road connections and later the automobile made such minor waypoints economically obsolete. Today the Arapahoe area of Colorado is agricultural plains with no surviving commercial infrastructure from the original community. The high plains around the historic Arapahoe community location, searched with landowner permission, can yield late 19th-century coins, homestead hardware, and cattle-drive era artifacts.

name: Arrow, CO
description: Arrow was a small Colorado mining or ranching community, established during the late 19th century in one of Colorado's many mountain or foothill valleys where mineral prospecting, ranching, or railroad construction created brief clusters of population. The community served as a minor local center before the resource that created it was exhausted or bypassed by other developments, leaving behind scattered structural ruins and mine workings. Today the Arrow community site is likely public or private land in Colorado's mountain terrain. Mine dump areas and old camp sites near the historic Arrow location can yield late 19th-century mining hardware, ranch implements, and domestic artifacts.

name: Corona, CO
description: Corona was a Colorado railroad pass community on the Continental Divide at the summit of Rollins Pass in Grand County, home to the Corona Station on the Denver, Northwestern and Pacific Railway — a remarkable engineering achievement that crossed the Continental Divide at 11,671 feet before the Moffat Tunnel was completed in 1928. The station featured a large depot building, hotel accommodations for stranded passengers, and facilities for the rotary snowplows needed to keep the high-altitude route open through brutal mountain winters. When the Moffat Tunnel allowed trains to pass under the Divide rather than over it, the Corona station was abandoned almost immediately. Today the Rollins Pass road and Corona area are accessible via a 4WD road from Winter Park or Rollinsville, with the old depot foundation and nearby ruins visible amid the tundra environment.

name: Autobees, CO
description: Autobees was a Colorado ranching and adobe trading post community on the Huerfano River in Huerfano County, established in the 1850s by Charles Autobee — a French-Canadian mountain man who built one of the earliest ranches on the Colorado plains and traded with both Ute and Hispanic communities across the region. The ranch served as a crucial waypoint on the Taos-to-Pueblo trade route and sheltered travelers, traders, and buffalo hunters during the turbulent era of the 1850s–1870s when Ute conflict and settler expansion reshaped the southern Colorado landscape. The Autobee ranch site has been identified archaeologically but is on private land in Huerfano County. Spanish colonial trade items, early American frontier hardware, and buffalo hunting equipment are the artifact types associated with this important pre-settlement trading post site.

name: Axial, CO
description: Axial was a small Colorado ranching and farming community in Moffat County in the semi-arid basin country north of the Yampa River, established in the late 19th century by homesteaders and cattlemen who discovered the Axial Basin's relatively sheltered terrain and reliable water suitable for small-scale ranching operations. The community served surrounding ranch families with a church, school, and country store before rural depopulation through the 20th century reduced it to a memory. Today the Axial Basin area of Moffat County is ranch land with no surviving commercial infrastructure. The remote but accessible basin terrain, managed largely as BLM rangeland, may yield late 19th-century ranch hardware, homestead implements, and early coins for patient searchers.

name: Bakerville, CO
description: Bakerville was a small Colorado mining and railroad community in Clear Creek County along the east slope of the Continental Divide near the current I-70 corridor, serving miners and travelers during the 19th-century mining boom when the Clear Creek Canyon route was the primary artery to the Leadville and Georgetown silver districts. The community had a hotel, saloon, and facilities serving the miners and freighters crossing Loveland Pass before the railroad pushed through and later Interstate 70 was built through the same corridor. Today the Bakerville area near the current I-70 Exit 221 retains some historical traces, and the Clear Creek canyon has been extensively explored for gold placer deposits. Old mining camp areas and roadhouse sites in the Clear Creek corridor can yield 19th-century travel hardware, miner's tools, and period coins.

name: Querida, CO
description: Querida was a small silver mining camp in Custer County, Colorado, situated in the Wet Mountains east of Westcliffe during the silver boom of the 1880s when the region attracted prospectors seeking extension of the Leadville-district silver formations. The camp had a post office, a general store, and worker housing at its peak, serving the miners who worked the local silver lodes before ore depletion and the silver crash of 1893 terminated operations. Today the Querida area is accessible via county roads south of Westcliffe, with old mine workings and scattered building remnants visible across the Wet Mountain terrain. The mine dumps and old camp sites around Querida can yield silver ore specimens, miner's tools, and period camp hardware for detectorists exploring the Custer County backcountry.

name: Bijou Basin, CO
description: Bijou Basin was a Colorado ranching community in Elbert County on the eastern plains east of Denver, established during the great cattle ranching era of the 1870s–1880s when longhorn cattle driven north from Texas grazed the rich shortgrass prairie of the Colorado high plains before barbed wire and homesteaders transformed the landscape. The community served as a minor center for surrounding ranches with a post office and gathering point, thriving during the open range era before the fencing of the plains and drought cycles through the early 20th century ended the cattle baron era. Today the Bijou Basin area is active ranching and farming land in Elbert County. Old ranch headquarters sites and cattle-era camps in the area may yield late 19th-century cowboy artifacts — spurs, cinch rings, branding hardware — for detectorists with landowner permission.

name: Bonanza, CO
description: Bonanza was a silver mining camp in Saguache County, Colorado, established in 1880 when prospectors flooded the Kerber Creek drainage of the San Luis Valley's eastern margins following significant silver discoveries, supporting a population of several hundred miners and a commercial district with hotels, saloons, and a smelter during its brief but intense peak. The ore played out rapidly, and the camp declined through the 1880s–1890s, though some mining activity continued sporadically through the early 20th century. Today Bonanza is a small private community with a handful of residents and historic building remains, accessible from Moffat via county road over the mountains. The old townsite and mine dump areas around Bonanza can yield silver ore specimens, mining tokens, and period hardware for detectorists who coordinate with current property owners.

name: Bowerman, CO
description: Bowerman was a small Colorado mining or ranching community, established during the late 19th-century development of Colorado's mountain or foothill country and serving the local resource extraction economy for a generation before economic exhaustion or geographic isolation doomed it. The community had basic service facilities — a store, perhaps a church — before being abandoned when the economic rationale for its existence disappeared. Today the Bowerman site is likely on public lands in Colorado's mountain terrain or private ranch land in one of the state's many intermountain valleys. Old camp sites and mine workings near the historic Bowerman location may yield late 19th-century Colorado frontier artifacts.

name: Brodhead, CO
description: Brodhead was a small Colorado community on the eastern plains, possibly a minor railroad station or farming settlement established during the agricultural and cattle ranching development of Colorado's eastern plains in the 1880s–1900s. The community served surrounding ranch and farm interests as a minor local center before the rural depopulation of the Great Plains through the Dust Bowl and mid-20th century reduced it to a name on old maps. Today the area is active Colorado agricultural land. Old homestead sites and railroad station locations in the eastern Colorado plains can yield late 19th-century to early 20th-century coins, homestead hardware, and ranch implements for patient searchers.

name: Buckskin Joe, CO
description: Buckskin Joe was a significant gold and silver mining camp in Park County, Colorado, established in 1859 during the Pike's Peak gold rush and growing rapidly into one of the largest communities in the Colorado territory during the early 1860s, with thousands of miners, a courthouse, several hotels, and a regular stagecoach connection to Denver. The camp was named for Joseph Higginbottom, nicknamed 'Buckskin Joe' for his frontier attire, and served as a supply hub for the surrounding silver and gold claims in the Mosquito Range. By the 1870s the ore had declined, the county seat was moved to Fairplay, and most residents departed — leaving behind only scattered structural ruins. A historic reconstruction called 'Buckskin Joe' exists as a tourist attraction near Canon City (not the original site), while the actual historic townsite near Alma retains archaeological significance accessible on public lands.

name: Calcite, CO
description: Calcite was a small Colorado quarrying community in Garfield County, established around deposits of calcite or limestone used for industrial purposes such as flux in smelting operations or road-building material during the mining era of the late 19th and early 20th centuries. The community served the quarry workers and their families as a company camp before the deposits were exhausted or transportation changes made the operation uneconomical. Today the Calcite area is likely BLM or Forest Service land in western Colorado, with quarry workings and camp ruins still potentially visible in the terrain. Old quarry sites and camp areas in the region may yield early 20th-century industrial hardware, quarry tools, and domestic artifacts.

name: Calumet, CO
description: Calumet was a small Colorado mining community, likely associated with copper or coal mining given the name's strong association with the Calumet copper district of Michigan — a name that miners and promoters applied to other mining areas across the West in hopes of replicating Michigan's legendary copper wealth. The camp served surrounding mining interests during the late 19th or early 20th century before ore depletion or economic pressures ended operations. Today the Calumet site in Colorado is likely on public lands or private mining claim land with mine shaft collars and tailings piles as the most visible remnants. Old mine dumps and camp areas near the historic Calumet location can yield mining hardware, blasting equipment fragments, and period domestic items.

name: Capitol City, CO
description: Capitol City was an audaciously named silver mining camp in Hinsdale County, Colorado, situated in the high alpine terrain of the San Juan Mountains along Henson Creek at over 10,000 feet elevation, booming in the 1870s–1880s when silver ore discoveries led founder George Lee to christen it with a name reflecting his grand ambitions to make it the state capital. At its peak Capitol City had a smelter, several stores, a post office, and over 400 residents, with Lee personally constructing a brick governor's mansion anticipating the community's elevation to political prominence that never came. The silver crash of 1893 ended the dream abruptly, and the population scattered, leaving the governor's mansion and other structures to the elements. Today the Capitol City site is accessible via the Alpine Loop scenic byway north of Lake City, with foundation ruins and mine workings visible in the spectacular mountain setting.

name: Caribou, CO
description: Caribou was a significant silver mining camp in Boulder County, Colorado, established in 1869 after rich silver lodes were discovered in the mountains west of Nederland at 10,000 feet elevation, growing rapidly to over 3,000 residents during the 1870s–1880s with multiple stamp mills, a hotel, saloons, and a school serving the high-altitude mining community. Caribou silver went to some of the most sophisticated Colorado refineries, and the camp attracted Dutch investment that funded major mine operations throughout the boom decade. A series of devastating fires swept through the wooden buildings in the 1870s–1880s, and the silver crash of 1893 ended what remained of the community. Today the Caribou townsite is on Boulder County open space land and accessible on foot from Nederland, with foundation ruins and mine workings scattered across the alpine tundra above treeline.

name: Petersburg, IL
description: Petersburg was a small community in Illinois, distinct from the county seat of Menard County of the same name, likely a minor crossroads farming settlement or rural trading post established in the 19th-century agricultural settlement of the Illinois prairie that was eventually absorbed into or overshadowed by larger nearby communities. The community served surrounding farm families with a church and country store at its peak before economic consolidation drew its population to better-served towns. Today the smaller Petersburg location is agricultural land with no surviving commercial infrastructure. Old church sites and farmstead perimeters near the historic community location may yield 19th-century coins, domestic items, and farm hardware.

name: Pinkney, TN\n
description: Pinkney was a small Tennessee rural community, likely established during the early settlement of Middle Tennessee in the early 19th century by farm families growing cotton or tobacco on the rich limestone-underlain soils of the region. The community served surrounding agricultural interests with a church, school, and possibly a general store or cotton gin before being eclipsed by neighboring centers with better transportation connections. Today the Pinkney area is private Tennessee agricultural land with no surviving commercial structures. Old church lots and farmstead perimeters near the historic Pinkney community location may yield early 19th-century domestic artifacts, antebellum coins, and agricultural hardware.

name: Casaba, AZ
description: Casaba was a small community in Maricopa or Pinal County, Arizona, likely a minor agricultural or ranching settlement established during the late 19th or early 20th century as American farmers and ranchers moved into the desert Southwest following the pacification of Apache resistance and the extension of railroads into the region. The community served surrounding desert agricultural interests before the harsh desert climate and limited water resources made sustained commercial development impossible. Today the Casaba site is likely desert BLM or private ranch land in central Arizona. The dry desert environment preserves artifacts well, and old ranch sites and farming operations near the historic Casaba location may yield late 19th-century domestic items, ranch hardware, and early American coins.

name: Cordes, AZ
description: Cordes was a small Arizona community along Antelope Creek in Yavapai County, established as a stage stop and ranching center on the route between Prescott and Phoenix during the late 19th century before the railroad routes through the region changed commercial patterns. The Cordes family established the original ranch and stage stop that gave the community its name, and the area developed into a minor rural center for surrounding cattle ranchers working the Bradshaw Mountain foothills. Today the Cordes area survives as the tiny community of Cordes Junction on Interstate 17, a major Arizona highway, with the original stage stop site preserved nearby. The old ranch and stage stop areas near Cordes can yield late 19th-century travel hardware, ranch implements, and domestic items from the stage route era.

name: Middleton, AZ
description: Middleton was a small Arizona community in Yavapai County, established during the agricultural and ranching development of central Arizona's mountain country in the late 19th or early 20th century as the region's water resources were harnessed for small-scale irrigation farming and cattle ranching. The community served surrounding agricultural and ranching interests as a minor local center before being absorbed into the larger Prescott regional economy or simply fading from commercial significance. Today the Middleton area of Yavapai County is ranch land or small-scale agricultural terrain. Old ranch homestead sites and farmstead perimeters near the historic Middleton community may yield late 19th-century to early 20th-century Arizona frontier artifacts.

name: Hansen, AZ
description: Hansen was a small Arizona community, likely a minor ranching or agricultural settlement in the territorial era of the late 19th or early 20th century named for a Scandinavian settler family — the Hansen surname being common among Danish and Norwegian immigrants who settled across the American West during this period. The community served surrounding ranch families as a minor local gathering point before being absorbed into the larger Arizona territorial economy or simply fading when its initial population moved on. Today the Hansen community site in Arizona is likely private ranch land. Old ranch homestead sites near the historic Hansen location may yield late 19th-century domestic hardware, ranch implements, and Scandinavian-American cultural artifacts.

name: Trenton, KS
description: Trenton was a small Kansas farming community established during the great Kansas settlement era of the 1870s–1890s, when thousands of homestead families claimed the rich wheat-growing land of the central Kansas plains after the removal of Native American nations and the extension of the railroads. The community had a grain elevator, church, and general store at its height before the combined forces of drought, falling wheat prices, and rural depopulation through the early-to-mid 20th century emptied it. Today the Trenton area is active Kansas agricultural land, with perhaps a deteriorating grain elevator as the last standing structure. Old elevator sites and homestead perimeters in the area may yield late 19th-century to early 20th-century coins, farm implements, and domestic artifacts.

name: Lake Valley, NM
description: Lake Valley was a significant silver mining camp in Sierra County, New Mexico, booming in the 1880s when the Bridal Chamber mine — one of the richest single ore pockets ever discovered in the American Southwest — yielded enormous quantities of nearly pure silver chloride ore, reportedly enough that refined silver bars were used as floor tiles in the mine office. The town grew to over 4,000 residents with hotels, saloons, multiple stores, and a railroad depot before the silver crashed in 1893 and the Bridal Chamber played out, emptying the camp within a decade. Today Lake Valley is a remarkable ghost town accessible via a dirt road south of Hillsboro, New Mexico, managed jointly by the Bureau of Land Management, with a preserved depot, schoolhouse, and assay office still standing. The mine dump areas and old townsite are accessible for exploration, with silver ore specimens and mining camp artifacts among the possible finds.

name: Nutt, NM
description: Nutt was a tiny New Mexico community in Luna County along the Southern Pacific Railroad line, established as a minor railroad station and ranching center serving the sparse pastoral country of southwestern New Mexico's high desert during the late 19th and early 20th centuries. The community had a post office and served as the nearest railroad access point for ranchers working the surrounding desert range, but never developed beyond a station-level settlement. Today Nutt is largely abandoned, with a few deteriorating structures remaining along the old highway and railroad alignment south of Hatch. The desert environment preserves artifacts well, and the old station area and surrounding ranch sites may yield late 19th-century railroad hardware, ranch implements, and period domestic items.

name: Terra Cotta, KS
description: Terra Cotta was a small Kansas community, established during the late 19th or early 20th century and possibly associated with clay products manufacturing — terra cotta being fired clay used for decorative building elements and pottery. The community served surrounding agricultural and possibly light industrial interests before being absorbed into the larger Kansas rural economy or simply becoming inactive as its commercial reason for existing faded. Today Terra Cotta is a historical name in Kansas records. Old kiln sites and associated community perimeters near the historic Terra Cotta location in Kansas may yield early 20th-century ceramic fragments, industrial hardware, and domestic items.

name: Ocoya, IL
description: Ocoya was a small community in Livingston County, Illinois, established during the mid-to-late 19th century as a minor farming and railway community along one of the rail lines crossing the Illinois prairie, serving surrounding grain farmers with a small commercial district that included a grain elevator, general store, and church. The community faded through the 20th century as rural populations consolidated in larger nearby Livingston County centers like Pontiac and Flanagan. Today Ocoya is a small unincorporated community with minimal remaining commercial activity. Old grain elevator foundations and farmstead perimeters near the historic Ocoya community can yield late 19th-century coins, agricultural hardware, and domestic items.

name: Selbytown, IL\n
description: Selbytown was a small Illinois farming community, likely established during the mid-to-late 19th century agricultural settlement of the state and named for the Selby family — a surname common among English-descended settlers who moved into Illinois from Ohio and Indiana during the great prairie farming expansion. The community served surrounding farm families as a minor rural center with a church and country store before rural consolidation drew its residents toward larger Illinois towns. Today Selbytown exists only in historical records. Old church sites and road traces near the historic Selbytown community location may yield 19th-century domestic artifacts and early American coins.

name: Rosati, MO
description: Rosati was a small Italian immigrant farming community in Phelps County, Missouri, established in the early 20th century by Italian immigrants — primarily from the Trentino and Piedmont regions — who were recruited to work in the vineyards and winery operations of the Missouri wine country. The community developed its own church, school, and Italian-language cultural institutions, making it one of the more distinctive ethnic farming enclaves in rural Missouri. The winery operations that anchored the community declined through Prohibition and later economic pressures, though some families remained. Old winery foundations and Italian-American community sites near Rosati may yield early 20th-century domestic items, wine-making hardware, and Italian-American cultural artifacts.

name: St. Thomas, NV
description: St. Thomas was a Nevada community in Clark County, situated at the confluence of the Muddy and Virgin Rivers in the Moapa Valley, established in 1865 by Mormon settlers as a farming and trade community that served the region for over 70 years before being permanently inundated by the rising waters of Lake Mead following the completion of Hoover Dam in 1938. The town had a post office, general store, hotel, and school, and its 500+ residents were the last to leave before the floodwaters rose — one family famously departed by boat from their own front porch. During drought years when Lake Mead drops significantly, the foundations of St. Thomas re-emerge from the lakebed, drawing photographers and history enthusiasts to the exposed ruins. Low-water periods offer rare opportunities to walk the old streets and examine the exposed rock and adobe foundations, with pottery shards, glass, and hardware occasionally found by careful searchers.

name: Hart, CA
description: Hart was a California desert community in San Bernardino County near the Nevada border, established in the early 20th century as a gold mining camp in the Providence Mountains when significant gold deposits were discovered in the remote Mojave Desert. The camp had a post office, several stores, and a population of several hundred miners at its peak during the 1910s–1920s before the gold played out and the extreme desert isolation made further operations uneconomical. Today the Hart area is within or adjacent to the Mojave National Preserve, managed by the National Park Service, with some mining ruins visible in the desert. Artifact collecting is prohibited within National Park Service boundaries, but the surrounding desert terrain outside the preserve may be accessible for exploration.

name: Juan, NV
description: Juan was a small Nevada mining community or ranch settlement, established during the late 19th or early 20th century in one of Nevada's many remote valleys or mountain ranges where mineral prospecting, ranching, or water access created brief clusters of population. The community served as a minor local center before economic or resource depletion dissolved it, leaving only scattered structural remains in the Nevada desert. Today the Juan community site is likely on BLM land in rural Nevada, with the dry desert environment preserving artifacts well. Old camp sites and mine workings near the historic Juan community location can yield late 19th-century Nevada frontier hardware and domestic items.

name: Sand Hill, MO
description: Sand Hill was a small Missouri farming community, established during the 19th century in the rolling Ozark margin country or Missouri river-bottom agricultural lands by farm families growing corn, tobacco, and livestock on the fertile Missouri soils. The community served surrounding agricultural families with a church, school, and country store, functioning as a minor rural center before improved transportation drew residents toward larger Missouri market towns. Today Sand Hill is absorbed into the surrounding Missouri agricultural landscape with no surviving commercial structures. Old church lots and farmstead perimeters near the historic Sand Hill community location can yield 19th-century coins, agricultural hardware, and domestic artifacts.

name: Bible Grove, MO
description: Bible Grove was a small Missouri community in Clark County in the Missouri 'boot heel' or northern Missouri region, organized around a church congregation as many 19th-century rural Missouri communities were, with a Bible-inspired name reflecting the evangelical Protestant culture that dominated the rural Midwest. The community served surrounding farm families with its church, school, and perhaps a country store during the 19th century before the usual pattern of rural consolidation and improved transportation dissolved the commercial core. Today Bible Grove exists only as a historical name in Missouri records. Old church sites and farmstead perimeters near the historic Bible Grove community location may yield 19th-century coins, religious community artifacts, and farm implements.

name: Yost, UT
description: Yost was a small ranching and farming community in Box Elder County, Utah, situated in the remote Raft River Valley country in the northwestern corner of the state near the Idaho border, established by homesteaders seeking the valley's reliable water and relatively sheltered terrain for grazing and small-scale irrigation farming. The community had a church, school, and small store serving the scattered ranching families of the area, persisting through the mid-20th century before rural depopulation emptied it. Today Yost is largely inactive, with a handful of structures remaining in the remote valley. The isolated Box Elder County terrain near Yost, accessible via county roads, may yield late 19th-century ranch hardware, homestead implements, and early 20th-century coins.

name: Soldier Summit, UT
description: Soldier Summit was a railroad community in Utah County, Utah, situated atop the dramatic divide between the Wasatch Front and the Uinta Basin at 7,477 feet elevation where the Denver and Rio Grande Western Railroad's mountain mainline crested the Wasatch Plateau — a brutal climb that required helper engines to push heavy freight trains up the grade during the steam era. The community housed railroad maintenance workers and their families in a cluster of company buildings perched on the summit, and it served the helper locomotive operations that were required on the steep grades through the 1940s–1950s. When diesel engines replaced steam and made helper operations less necessary, the community rapidly emptied. Today Soldier Summit retains a few deteriorating structures along US-6 in eastern Utah, and the railroad grade areas near the summit may yield railroad hardware and worker-era domestic artifacts.

name: Pickelville, UT
description: Pickelville was a small community in Rich County, Utah, situated near the shores of Bear Lake on the Utah-Idaho border, established by early Mormon settlers in the late 19th century who farmed the Bear Lake Valley's rich bottomland and fished the lake's renowned Bonneville cisco runs. The community served as a minor local center for the Bear Lake area during the late 19th and early 20th centuries before tourism development transformed the area's economy and the original agricultural community faded. Today the Bear Lake area is a popular Utah resort destination, and the Pickelville area retains some rural character amid the growing lake town. Old farmstead sites near the historic Pickelville community location can yield late 19th-century domestic artifacts and Mormon pioneer-era implements.

name: Bingham Canyon, UT
description: Bingham Canyon was one of the most significant mining communities in American history, a Utah copper mining town in Salt Lake County built into the steep walls of Bingham Canyon southwest of Salt Lake City that grew from an 1863 gold placer camp into a full-scale company town of 15,000 residents — one of the most densely populated mining towns in the Mountain West — serving the workers of the Kennecott Copper Mine. The town had multiple schools, ethnic-specific churches and commercial districts for its diverse immigrant population of Greeks, Italians, Finns, Japanese, and dozens of other nationalities who came to work the massive open-pit mine. Beginning in the 1950s, the expanding Kennecott open pit consumed the town section by section until the last residents were relocated in 1971 and the entire townsite was eaten by the mine. Today Bingham Canyon is entirely within the Kennecott Copper Mine property — the largest man-made excavation on Earth — and is not accessible to the public, though the overlook visitor center provides views of the pit where the town once stood.

name: Ile aux Marins, St. Pierre and Miquelon
description: Île aux Marins was a small fishing community on the French overseas territory of Saint-Pierre and Miquelon off the southern coast of Newfoundland, inhabited from the 16th century by Basque, Breton, and Norman fishermen who used the island as a base for the extraordinarily rich Grand Banks cod fishery that fueled European economies for centuries. The island community had a church, school, and the full infrastructure of a permanent fishing village, supporting over 600 residents at its peak in the late 19th century. The collapse of the cod fishery through the 20th century, combined with the practical advantages of living on the larger main island of Saint-Pierre, led to the gradual evacuation of Île aux Marins, with the last permanent residents leaving in 1965. Today the island is a preserved open-air museum with the old village intact, accessible by boat from Saint-Pierre, offering an extraordinary window into centuries of North Atlantic fishing culture.

name: Normantown, IL
description: Normantown was a small Illinois farming community in Greene County, established during the mid-19th century agricultural settlement of the Illinois River valley country by farm families growing corn, wheat, and hogs on the rich bottomland and upland soils of western Illinois. The community served surrounding farming interests with a church and store before being eclipsed by neighboring Illinois River towns with better transportation connections. Today Normantown is a small unincorporated community with minimal surviving commercial activity from the original settlement era. Old farmstead perimeters and road traces near the historic Normantown community in Greene County, Illinois can yield 19th-century coins, farm implements, and domestic artifacts.

name: Wolfs, IL
description: Wolfs was a small Illinois farming community, likely named for the Wolf or Wolff family — a German-American surname common among the German immigrant farm families who settled heavily in Illinois from the 1840s through the 1870s, establishing a strong presence in the central and southern Illinois agricultural landscape. The community served surrounding German-American farm families with a Lutheran or Catholic church and country store before rural consolidation through the 20th century absorbed it into the larger township structure. Today Wolfs exists only in old Illinois county records. German-American church sites and farmstead perimeters near the historic Wolfs community location can yield 19th-century domestic items, German-American cultural artifacts, and farm implements.

name: Ingham, OH
description: Ingham was a small Ohio farming community in one of the state's agricultural counties, established during the 19th century settlement of Ohio's interior by farming families who claimed the rich glacially deposited soils and built small rural communities with churches, schools, and country stores to serve their agricultural needs. The community declined as improved transportation and rural consolidation through the late 19th and 20th centuries drew its residents toward larger Ohio centers. Today the Ingham community is absorbed into Ohio's agricultural landscape with no surviving commercial structures. Old church lots and farmstead perimeters near the historic Ingham community location in Ohio may yield 19th-century coins, domestic ceramics, and agricultural hardware.

name: Aaronsburg, OH
description: Aaronsburg was a small Ohio rural community, distinct from the more notable Aaronsburg in Pennsylvania, established by Scots-Irish or German settlers during Ohio's rapid agricultural development in the early-to-mid 19th century. The community served surrounding farming families with a church and a country store before rural consolidation and improved transportation drew its residents toward larger Ohio centers through the late 19th century. Today the community has faded from active commercial life, leaving only old county records to document its history. Old church sites and farmstead perimeters near the historic Aaronsburg community location in Ohio can yield 19th-century domestic artifacts, early American coins, and farm implements.

name: Rome, Morrow County, OH
description: Rome in Morrow County was a small Ohio farming community established during the 19th century settlement of north-central Ohio, its Roman name reflecting the classically inspired naming fashion common among educated American settlers of the early 19th century who gave their communities names evoking the grandeur of the ancient world. The community had a church, school, and store at its height, serving the surrounding dairy and grain farming families of Morrow County before rural consolidation through the 20th century reduced it to a historical footnote. Today the Rome community site in Morrow County is surrounded by agricultural land. Old church and schoolhouse sites near the historic Rome community can yield 19th-century Ohio domestic artifacts and early American coins.

name: Alexandersville, OH
description: Alexandersville was a small Ohio community in Montgomery County, established during the early 19th century agricultural and mill-building era in the Miami River valley country of southwestern Ohio, likely centering on a water-powered mill that served surrounding grain farmers. The community had a brief period of commercial activity before being absorbed into the expanding Dayton metropolitan area through the 20th century. Today the Alexandersville area is suburban Montgomery County, with its 19th-century agricultural past buried under modern development. Original farmstead sites occasionally emerge during construction, sometimes revealing early Ohio domestic artifacts and 19th-century coins.

name: Allentown, Clark County, OH
description: Allentown in Clark County was a small Ohio farming community established during the early 19th century settlement of the Mad River valley country in southwestern Ohio, serving surrounding agricultural families with a church, school, and country store typical of the small Ohio rural communities that dotted the landscape before railroad-era consolidation. The community declined as improved transportation and growing urban centers like Springfield drew residents away through the mid-to-late 19th century. Today the Clark County area near the historic Allentown community is active Ohio agricultural land. Old church lots and farmstead perimeters in the area can yield early American coins, domestic ceramics, and farm implements.

name: Arrowsmiths, OH
description: Arrowsmiths was a small Ohio farming community, likely named for an early settler family involved in the blacksmithing trade — arrowsmiths being craftsmen who made iron arrowheads and later edged tools and farm implements. The community served surrounding farm families with a blacksmith shop, a church, and perhaps a small store before rural consolidation through the 19th century reduced it to a historical footnote. Today Arrowsmiths is absorbed into Ohio's agricultural landscape. Old blacksmith shop sites and farmstead perimeters near the historic Arrowsmiths community can yield 19th-century iron hardware, forge waste, and early American coins for detectorists with landowner permission.

name: Beta, OH
description: Beta was a small Ohio farming community, likely platted during the mid-19th century with a name drawn from the Greek alphabet — a naming convention occasionally used by educated settlers who sought distinctive and memorable community names that stood apart from the flood of Washington, Jefferson, and Millard-named Ohio towns. The community served surrounding farm families as a minor rural center before rural consolidation and improved transportation drew its residents toward larger Ohio centers. Today Beta exists only in old Ohio county records. Old church sites and road intersections near the historic Beta community location in Ohio can yield 19th-century domestic artifacts and early American coins.

name: Bethlehem, Marion County, OH
description: Bethlehem in Marion County was a small Ohio rural community established by religious settlers who chose the biblical name for their community in the tradition of Christian-named Ohio settlements that proliferated across the state during the evangelical Protestant settlement era of the early 19th century. The community served surrounding farming families with its church — the anchor of the settlement — along with a school and perhaps a general store before rural consolidation through the 19th century dissolved its commercial core. Today the Bethlehem community in Marion County is farmland with a possible historic cemetery as the only surviving physical evidence. Old church sites and farmstead perimeters near the historic Bethlehem community can yield early 19th-century Ohio domestic artifacts and coins.

name: San Toy, OH
description: San Toy was a coal mining community in Morgan County, Ohio, established in the early 20th century as a company town by the Sunday Creek Coal Company to house miners working the extensive coal seams of the Hocking Valley coalfield, one of the most productive coal-producing regions of eastern Ohio during the first half of the 20th century. The company town had hundreds of miners and their families, a company store, and ethnic diversity typical of Ohio coal camps — with large populations of Welsh, Italian, Eastern European, and Black miners. When the mines closed as cheaper sources of coal and then other fuels displaced Ohio's underground coal industry, the company town was rapidly abandoned. Today San Toy is a ghost community in Morgan County, its former streets and building foundations gradually being reclaimed by the Ohio forest, accessible on publicly managed land.

name: Flora, OH
description: Flora in Ohio was a small rural community, one of dozens of Ohio towns bearing this simple botanical name, established during the 19th century agricultural settlement of the state and serving surrounding farming families with the standard complement of church, school, and country store that characterized small Midwestern agricultural communities. The community declined through the late 19th and early 20th centuries as rural consolidation and improved transportation drew Ohio farmers toward larger centers. Today Flora in Ohio exists only in historical records. Old church sites and farmstead perimeters near the historic Flora community location in Ohio can yield 19th-century domestic artifacts, early American coins, and agricultural hardware.

name: Marengo, Lucas County, OH
description: Marengo in Lucas County was a small Ohio community in the northwestern Ohio lake plain country, established during the early-to-mid 19th century settlement of the Black Swamp region when extensive drainage works transformed the formerly impassable wetlands into productive agricultural land. The community served surrounding newly drained farmland with a church and store, benefiting from the agricultural boom that followed Black Swamp drainage before being eclipsed by larger Lucas County centers like Toledo. Today the area is active northwestern Ohio agricultural land. Old farmstead sites and drainage-era infrastructure near the historic Marengo community in Lucas County can yield early to mid-19th-century Ohio domestic artifacts and coins.

name: Providence, OH
description: Providence was a small Ohio community in Lucas County along the Maumee River in the Black Swamp country of northwestern Ohio, notable as the site of a historic canal lock on the Miami and Erie Canal — one of the engineering marvels of the 1840s that connected Lake Erie to the Ohio River and transformed Ohio's interior economy. The lock and associated canal community served as an important waypoint for the enormous volume of agricultural goods that moved through the Maumee Valley corridor during the canal era, before the railroads made the canals obsolete by the 1880s. Today the Providence area retains a restored canal lock operated as Farnsworth Metropark, managed by the Metroparks of the Toledo Area, giving visitors an authentic view of the canal era infrastructure. The area around the lock and former canal community is a fascinating historical site, though the park status limits metal detecting.

name: Middleburg, Seneca County, OH
description: Middleburg in Seneca County was a small Ohio farming community established during the early 19th century settlement of north-central Ohio's fertile lake plain and till plain country, serving surrounding agricultural families with a church, school, and country store before being eclipsed by neighboring Seneca County centers with railroad connections. The community had a modest period of commercial activity during the first half of the 19th century before rural consolidation through the latter half reduced it to a historical footnote. Today the Middleburg community in Seneca County is Ohio agricultural land with no surviving commercial infrastructure. Old church and schoolhouse sites near the historic Middleburg community location can yield early 19th-century Ohio domestic artifacts and coins.

name: Hopewell, OH
description: Hopewell in Ohio was a small rural community bearing one of the state's most evocative names — a reference to the Hopewell culture, the remarkable prehistoric Native American civilization that built hundreds of earthwork mounds across the Ohio valley from roughly 100 BCE to 500 CE. The farming community established in the area during the 19th century was likely near or adjacent to these ancient earthworks, and its name may have been chosen in recognition of the visible archaeological heritage surrounding the settlement. The community served surrounding Ohio farm families in the 19th century before rural consolidation dissolved its commercial core. Historic Hopewell culture earthwork sites across Ohio are managed as protected archaeological resources — detectorists must avoid such sites — but surrounding farmland may yield 19th-century Ohio domestic artifacts.

name: Murraysville, OH
description: Murraysville was a small Ohio community in Washington or another Ohio county, established during the early 19th-century settlement of the Ohio hill country by farming and milling families who sought the water power of the region's creek drainages for sawmilling and gristmilling operations alongside agricultural development. The community had a mill, a store, and a church at its height, serving the surrounding agricultural families before rural consolidation and improved transportation drew residents toward larger centers. Today Murraysville is absorbed into the surrounding Ohio landscape. Old mill pond sites and farmstead perimeters near the historic Murraysville community can yield 19th-century millwright hardware, early Ohio coins, and domestic artifacts.

name: Brandywine Village, OH
description: Brandywine Village was a small Ohio community named for the Brandywine River — a name shared by streams across the eastern United States honoring the historic creek in Delaware where Washington's Continental Army fought in 1777. The Ohio settlement likely served surrounding agricultural families in the hilly country of northeastern Ohio with a mill, church, and small store during the 19th century. As improved transportation and industrial growth concentrated Ohio's population in larger centers, Brandywine Village faded from commercial significance. Old mill sites and church lots near the historic Brandywine Village community location in Ohio can yield early-to-mid 19th-century domestic artifacts, mill hardware, and coins.

name: Cobbs Corners, OH
description: Cobbs Corners was a small Ohio farming community named for a settler family named Cobb who established the original crossroads store or tavern that anchored the community's commercial life during the 19th century. The community served surrounding Ohio farm families as a minor local center before rural consolidation and improved transportation through the late 19th and 20th centuries drew residents toward larger Ohio centers. Today Cobbs Corners is absorbed into Ohio's agricultural landscape. Old road intersection sites and farmstead perimeters near the historic Cobbs Corners community location can yield 19th-century Ohio domestic artifacts, early American coins, and farm hardware.

name: Butterworth, OH
description: Butterworth was a small Ohio farming community, likely named for an early settler family — the Butterworth surname being a common English-origin name among Ohio's early settlers from New England and New York who established farming communities across the state's interior during the 1810s–1840s. The community served surrounding farm families with a church and store, functioning as a minor rural gathering point before improved transportation and rural consolidation dissolved its commercial core. Today Butterworth exists only in old Ohio county records and plat maps. Old church sites and farmstead perimeters near the historic Butterworth community location in Ohio can yield early 19th-century coins, domestic ceramics, and farm implements.

name: Simpkinsville, OH
description: Simpkinsville was a small Ohio community named for the Simpkins family, established during the 19th century settlement of Ohio's interior as a minor rural center serving surrounding farming families with a church, school, and country store. The community had a brief but genuine commercial existence before being absorbed into the larger township structure as rural Ohio populations consolidated through the latter half of the 19th century and into the 20th. Today Simpkinsville is a historical name in old Ohio plat records. Old church lots and farmstead perimeters near the historic Simpkinsville community location in Ohio may yield 19th-century domestic artifacts, coins, and agricultural hardware.

name: Kelsey, OH
description: Kelsey was a small Ohio farming community, likely named for a settler family who were among the early arrivals in the area during Ohio's rapid agricultural settlement in the first half of the 19th century. The community served surrounding agricultural families with the standard rural combination of church, school, and general store before being eclipsed by better-connected neighboring Ohio communities through the latter half of the 19th century. Today Kelsey is absorbed into Ohio's agricultural landscape with no surviving commercial infrastructure. Farm field margins and old road traces near the historic Kelsey community location in Ohio can yield 19th-century coins, domestic artifacts, and farm implements for detectorists with landowner permission.

name: Three Legs Town, OH
description: Three Legs Town was a small Ohio community with an unusual name that may derive from a tavern sign — tavern signs depicting animals or human figures with distinctive features were common commercial identifiers in the pre-literacy era, and a three-legged animal or figure would have been a memorable and distinctive landmark along an early Ohio road. The community served passing travelers and surrounding farm families as a minor wayside stop during the early 19th century, declining as improved roads and later turnpikes shifted traffic to better-maintained routes. Today Three Legs Town exists only as a curiosity in Ohio historical records, its precise location and final fate obscured by two centuries of agricultural transformation. Old road trace areas and historic tavern site locations in the surrounding Ohio countryside can yield early American coins, travel hardware, and tavern-era domestic artifacts.

name: Rossville, Knox County, OH
description: Rossville was a modest rural village platted in the early 1800s in Knox County, Ohio, serving as a local trading center for surrounding farmsteads along the rich agricultural flatlands of central Ohio. Like many small Ohio hamlets, the community faded when the county road network shifted and nearby larger towns drew commerce away by the late 19th century. Today Rossville exists in name only, with former lots now absorbed into surrounding farm fields. The surrounding countryside, with its deep history of early settlement, can yield nineteenth-century farm tokens, harness hardware, and domestic artifacts for detectorists working nearby field edges.

name: Linton Mills, OH
description: Linton Mills was an early industrial settlement in Guernsey County, Ohio, built around a grist and saw mill operation that served surrounding farming communities in the mid-1800s. The mill drew settlers and small commerce, but as regional milling consolidated into larger operations and railroads bypassed the site, the community dwindled and eventually disappeared. Little remains above ground today, though the general area retains its rural character. Mill sites like this one are productive detecting locations, often yielding cut nails, millstone fragments, early coins, and domestic hardware from the active settlement period.

name: Central City, OH
description: Central City was a small agricultural community in Licking County, Ohio that emerged in the early settlement era as farmers cleared the dense hardwood forests of central Ohio. The town platted optimistically but never grew beyond a handful of structures, losing population steadily as neighboring Newark absorbed local commerce and services through the 19th century. The original townsite has long since returned to farmland, leaving no visible structures. Metal detectorists searching nearby field edges may encounter early Ohio land sale tokens, ox and horse hardware, and lost coins from the community's brief active period.

name: Dicksonton, OH
description: Dicksonton was a forgotten rural hamlet in Perry County, Ohio, platted during the mid-1800s land rush that created dozens of aspirational but short-lived communities across the hilly Appalachian foothills of southeastern Ohio. The settlement likely served as a local crossroads with a general store and possibly a blacksmith, but never attracted the railroad connection needed for sustained growth. Today the area is characterized by second-growth forest and old pasture, typical of Ohio's Hocking Hills region. Detectorists who gain permission to search former homestead sites nearby can find period buttons, boot buckles, and copper coinage from the antebellum era.

name: Oreton, OH
description: Oreton was a small settlement in Gallia County, Ohio that grew as farmers and early industrialists exploited the timber and modest mineral resources of the unglaciated Appalachian plateau of southern Ohio in the mid-19th century. The community had enough residents to support a post office for a period, but declining timber resources and the pull of larger Ohio River towns eventually emptied the settlement by the early 20th century. The rugged terrain around the former townsite is now wooded second growth. Careful prospecting of old homestead clearings in the area can uncover period iron hardware, wire-cut nails, and occasional silver coins.

name: Richland, OH
description: Richland was a small farming community in Gallia County in the rolling hill country of southernmost Ohio, founded during the early 19th century wave of Virginian and Kentuckian settlers crossing the Ohio River seeking farmland. The community sustained a brief period of activity with a church and school before better road and rail connections drew population to larger centers. Today the original settlement area is largely absorbed into agricultural land. The rich cultural landscape of southern Gallia County, settled early and continuously farmed, offers detectorists productive searching near old fence lines and home sites.

name: Moonville, OH
description: Moonville was a remote coal mining hamlet in Vinton County, Ohio carved out of the dense Raccoon Creek valley woodlands in the 1850s to house workers serving the Marietta & Cincinnati Railroad. At its peak the community had a few dozen residents including miners, railroad workers, and their families clustered around a coal tipple and company housing. When the mine played out in the 1870s and the railroad rerouted, Moonville was quickly abandoned, leaving behind its now-famous arched stone tunnel as the sole lasting landmark. Today the Moonville Tunnel is an iconic hiking destination in the Wayne National Forest, and while the site is publicly accessible, artifact removal is prohibited within the forest boundary.

name: Claysburg, OH
description: Claysburg was a small rural community in Preble County in the fertile Till Plains of western Ohio, platted in the early 19th century to serve farming families settling the exceptionally rich glacial soils of the region. The settlement never secured a railroad connection and steadily lost residents to nearby Eaton and other growing county seat towns through the latter half of the 1800s. The original plat area is now completely absorbed into the surrounding agricultural landscape. The farmland heritage of Preble County — one of Ohio's earliest settled western counties — makes surrounding field edges productive terrain for finding early Ohio land tokens, harness fittings, and silver coinage.

name: Venable, OH
description: Venable was a hamlet in Warren County, Ohio located in the prosperous agricultural region between the Little Miami and Great Miami Rivers, settled in the early decades of the 19th century by farmers drawn to the fertile glacial soils. The community briefly had a post office and school but was ultimately absorbed into the orbit of nearby Lebanon as transportation improved. No structures remain above ground at the original site. The rich agricultural history of Warren County, one of southwestern Ohio's most densely settled early counties, makes former homestead sites productive for finding domestic artifacts, buttons, and coins from the antebellum period.

name: Manor, OH
description: Manor was a small rural community in Warren County, Ohio situated near the agricultural heartland of southwestern Ohio, platted during the early 19th century land rush following the opening of the Virginia Military District. The settlement had enough activity to warrant a post office listing before consolidating into larger nearby communities as the county's road network improved. Today the site is indistinguishable from the surrounding productive farmland. Warren County's long continuous agricultural history, dating to Revolutionary War land grants, makes its former homestead sites productive locations for finding early American coinage, military buttons, and domestic hardware.

name: Fishburg, OH
description: Fishburg was a small milling and farming community in Montgomery County, Ohio established in the early 1800s along a tributary creek that provided water power for an early grist mill serving the surrounding rural neighborhood. The community never grew beyond a handful of structures and was largely absorbed into the expanding agricultural landscape of the Miami Valley by the mid-19th century. No physical traces of the original settlement remain visible today. The Miami Valley, one of Ohio's most historically significant agricultural regions, contains numerous former homestead sites where patient detectorists can find pioneer-era coins, domestic hardware, and early Ohio territorial artifacts.

name: Tadmor, OH
description: Tadmor was a small agricultural village in Montgomery County, Ohio platted during the early settlement era when Ohio developers named their towns after ancient and exotic places in hopes of attracting ambitious settlers. The community had a brief post office and served as a local crossroads before Better road connections and the expansion of nearby cities drew its population away by the latter half of the 19th century. The original townsite is now engulfed by suburban expansion south of Dayton. Montgomery County's layered settlement history, from early pioneers through the Civil War era, has left numerous domestic artifact deposits beneath old field edges and farmsteads.

name: Carpenter's Mill, OH
description: Carpenter's Mill was an early industrial site in Delaware County, Ohio where a water-powered mill complex served the surrounding farming communities of central Ohio's fertile glacial till plains during the first half of the 19th century. Mill sites like this drew blacksmiths, teamsters, and a small support community of merchants and laborers before steam power and improved roads made such rural operations obsolete. The original mill race and dam may still leave faint landscape traces, though the area is now farmland. Mill-era detecting around Delaware County can yield cut iron nails, mill tokens, wagon hardware, and copper coins from the active operation period.

name: Cones Mills, OH
description: Cones Mills was a small mill-based community in Union County, Ohio established along a creek drainage where early settlers built a grist and saw mill to serve the rapidly developing farmland of western central Ohio in the early 1800s. The settlement that grew around the mill was never formally platted and faded when the mill closed, leaving the area to revert to agricultural use. Union County's exceptional soil fertility made it a favored destination for early settlers, and the region is rich with buried artifact deposits from the pioneer era through the Civil War. Detectorists with landowner permission may find mill-era tokens, cut nails, and early American copper coins at former settlement sites.

name: Edinburgh, OH
description: Edinburgh was a small crossroads settlement in Union County, Ohio that served the busy agricultural community developing around the upper Scioto River watershed in the early decades of the 19th century. Named with Scottish flair by settlers of Scots-Irish descent, the community briefly had a tavern and general store before being bypassed by the railroad infrastructure that favored Marysville and other county towns. Today Edinburgh is a quiet rural neighborhood rather than an abandoned site, though its original commercial core has long vanished. The surrounding farmland retains strong archaeological potential for early Ohio settlement artifacts, particularly along field margins near old creek crossings.

name: Webster, Putnam County, OH
description: Webster was a small farming village in Putnam County, Ohio located in the Black Swamp region of northwest Ohio, an area that was among the last portions of Ohio to be settled due to its challenging drainage and dense wetland vegetation. Once drainage improvements made the land accessible in the mid-1800s, Putnam County was rapidly settled and dotted with small agricultural communities like Webster. The town faded as improved roads and railroads drew commerce to Ottawa, Leipsic, and Pandora. The rich dark soils of the former Black Swamp, among the most fertile in Ohio, harbor abundant early settlement artifacts for detectorists working with landowner permission.

name: White Town, OH
description: White Town was an informal rural community in Hardin County, Ohio that emerged during the mid-19th century settlement of the northwestern Ohio Black Swamp region, where ambitious drainage projects in the 1850s-1870s opened vast areas of former wetland to agriculture. The settlement was never formally platted and consisted primarily of farmsteads clustered around a local school or church. As the rural population consolidated through the early 20th century, White Town ceased to exist as a distinct community. Hardin County's agricultural landscape, settled intensively in the post-drainage era, holds productive detecting territory near old farmstead sites and abandoned school grounds.

name: Morehouse, OH
description: Morehouse was a small rural community in Knox County, Ohio situated in the rolling glaciated terrain of central Ohio's Kokosing River watershed, settled primarily by Pennsylvania German and Scots-Irish farming families in the early 19th century. The community had a sufficient population for a local school and church but was too small and too close to Mount Vernon to sustain independent commercial development. Today the original area blends seamlessly into the surrounding agricultural landscape. Knox County's deep pioneer heritage, including settlements dating to the Northwest Territory era, makes its farmland productive territory for finding early American coins, domestic buttons, and farm hardware.

name: Moshers Mill, OH
description: Moshers Mill was a small industrial hamlet in Knox County, Ohio built around a water-powered mill operation along a Kokosing River tributary in the early settlement era of central Ohio. The mill served surrounding farming communities through the antebellum period, but the industrial revolution and improved regional transportation made small rural mills obsolete by the Civil War era. The settlement dispersed, and the mill site eventually returned to agricultural and natural land use. Mill-based settlements in Knox County provide productive detecting terrain for finding early Ohio-era tokens, millstone hardware, iron tools, and antebellum coinage.

name: Steiners Corner, OH
description: Steiners Corner was a small agricultural crossroads community in Knox County, Ohio named for an early German-American settler family who established a farm and possibly a small store or tavern at a rural road junction in the early 1800s. Like dozens of similar eponymous communities across Ohio, Steiners Corner never grew beyond a local landmark designation, losing even that as its founding families moved on and the roads realigned. The central Ohio farmland surrounding this former crossroads, with its long continuous agricultural use since pioneer settlement, is productive territory for finding early 19th-century domestic artifacts, harness hardware, and American coinage.

name: Jamestown, Morrow County, OH
